frameworks.name: policy compression
frameworks.one_line: Capacity-constrained agents minimize mutual information between actions and states

frameworks.name: matching law
frameworks.one_line: Choice ratio matches reward ratio; explained via capacity limits

frameworks.name: rate-distortion
frameworks.one_line: Reward maximization traded against policy complexity cost

frameworks.name: dopaminergic modulation
frameworks.one_line: Dopamine level mapped to policy complexity capacity in Parkinson's

core_question: Why do agents systematically undermatch rather than match or overmatch in concurrent choice tasks?
key_assumptions: Agents jointly maximize reward and minimize policy complexity
Policy complexity = mutual information between actions and states
Cognitive cost is a capacity constraint on state-dependent policies
manipulations: Task conditions predicted to exaggerate undermatching tested in mice
Dopamine level varied in Parkinson's patients (medication state)
key_findings: Capacity-constrained agents can only undermatch or perfectly match, never overmatch
Mouse behavioral data validated a novel prediction about which conditions exaggerate undermatching
Higher dopamine levels in Parkinson's patients reduced undermatching
interpretations: Undermatching is a normative consequence of policy compression under capacity constraints
Dopamine modulates effective policy complexity capacity
connecting_signals: mutual information between actions and states
